message.content:
Adversarially verify this finding about a design doc (the doc is at /tmp/claude-1000/-home-jleechan-projects-worktree-runner/8ca589dd-8422-44b1-a543-52bd7b71f14b/scratchpad/gistfile1.txt; the repo is /home/jleechan/projects/worktree_runner; you may use WebSearch/WebFetch and read files). Try to REFUTE it. Finding from the "architecture" reviewer, severity critical:
TITLE: No runner registration/authentication design at all
DETAIL: The design never mentions how a runner becomes known to GitHub. There is no GitHub PAT or GitHub App credential flow, no JIT runner config, no registration-token acquisition or expiry handling (registration tokens are short-lived), and no repo/org/enterprise scoping decision. The generated outrunner.yml (src/config.rs, lines 212-251 of the doc) contains only backend/labels/count/resources — no `github:` section, no URL, no auth reference. Core Principle 3 says 'Use GitHub Scale Sets + ephemeral runners by default', which implies an auth-heavy control-plane integration, yet nothing in the module structure (section 4) owns credentials or registration. As written, `gha-isolated init && gha-isolated start` cannot produce a runner that ever appears in GitHub. This may be delegated to gha-outrunner, but the design never says so (see the 'unspecified responsibility boundary' finding) — a design for a runner tool that never utters the word 'token' is incomplete at its core.
RECOMMENDATION: Add an explicit auth section: choose GitHub App (installation token) as primary with PAT fallback; specify JIT runner config for ephemeral runners (per-job token, no persistent credential in the guest); define scoping (repo vs org) as a first-class `init` question; document token lifecycle (who mints registration tokens, how refresh works under a long-lived service); and define where the credential is stored (OS keychain / file with 0600 in the config dir — never in outrunner.yml or process env visible to jobs).
Is the finding factually correct and does the recommendation make sense? If the factual basis is wrong or the severity is inflated, refute it. Default to refuted=true only if you have concrete contrary evidence or the claim is unsupported after checking.

input.description: Search repo for outrunner and gha-isolated references

input.query: gha-outrunner GitHub Actions runner tool configuration

content.content:
Web search results for query: "gha-outrunner GitHub Actions runner tool configuration"

Links: [{"title":"GitHub - NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner: Ephemeral VM and container runners for GitHub Actions — no Kubernetes required","url":"https://github.com/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner"},{"title":"Get started with Actions Runner Controller - GitHub Docs","url":"https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/use-actions-runner-controller/get-started"},{"title":"Deploying runner scale sets with Actions Runner Controller - GitHub Docs","url":"https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/manage-runners/use-actions-runner-controller/deploy-runner-scale-sets"},{"title":"How to Configure Self-Hosted Runners in GitHub Actions","url":"https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-01-25-github-actions-self-hosted-runners/view"},{"title":"actions-runner-controller/charts/gha-runner-scale-set/values.yaml at master · actions/actions-runner-controller","url":"https://github.com/actions/actions-runner-controller/blob/master/charts/gha-runner-scale-set/values.yaml"},{"title":"Deploying Actions Runner Controller for Cost-Efficient GitHub Actions | by Amina lawal | HostSpace Cloud Solutions | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/hostspaceng/deploying-actions-runner-controller-for-cost-efficient-github-actions-bdbd7bf0549e"},{"title":"Actions Runner Controller release 0.14.0 - GitHub Changelog","url":"https://github.blog/changelog/2026-03-19-actions-runner-controller-release-0-14-0/"},{"title":"github-actions-runner | The Cloud Posse Reference Architecture","url":"https://docs.cloudposse.com/components/library/aws/eks/github-actions-runner/"},{"title":"GitHub - PasseiDireto/gh-runner: Self Hosted GitHub Actions Runner · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/PasseiDireto/gh-runner"},{"title":"GitHub - chaddyc/gha-runner: GitHub Actions Runner Docker Container · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/chaddyc/gha-runner"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about **gha-outrunner**:

## Overview

gha-outrunner provides ephemeral GitHub Actions runners with no Kubernetes required, provisioning fresh Docker containers or VMs for each GitHub Actions job, then destroying them when the job completes.

## Key Features

It uses GitHub's scaleset API to register as an autoscaling runner group. outrunner gives you isolation guarantees with Docker, libvirt, or Tart.

## How It Differs from ARC

Unlike GitHub's Actions Runner Controller (ARC) which requires Kubernetes, if you're running on bare metal or a simple VPS, you shouldn't need a cluster just to get ephemeral runners.

## Backend Support

The tool supports multiple provisioners:
- Container per job with fastest startup
- KVM VM from qcow2 golden image with CoW overlays using QEMU Guest Agent for command execution
- Tart VM clone per job with Tart guest agent for command execution

For detailed configuration documentation, see the provisioner reference for lifecycle details and runner image requirements. You can find the official repository at [github.com/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner](https://github.com/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner).


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

input.prompt: How does gha-outrunner authenticate with GitHub and register runners? What does its configuration file (outrunner.yml or similar) look like — does it require a github section, URL, token/PAT/GitHub App credentials? Quote the relevant config example and auth instructions.

content.content:
# gha-outrunner Authentication and Configuration

Based on the provided GitHub repository page, the specific details about authentication mechanisms and configuration file format are **not included in the content shown**.

The page confirms that gha-outrunner "uses GitHub's [scaleset API](https://github.com/actions/scaleset) to register as an autoscaling runner group," but it does not contain:

- Example configuration files (outrunner.yml or similar)
- Authentication requirements (PAT, GitHub App, tokens)
- GitHub credentials setup instructions
- Configuration syntax or structure

To find this information, you would need to:

1. **Check the full README.md** in the repository (linked but not fully displayed)
2. **Consult the [Configuration reference](/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner/blob/main/docs/reference/configuration.md)** (mentioned but content not provided)
3. **Review setup guides** like the [Ubuntu/Debian](/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner/blob/main/docs/setup/linux-deb.md) installation documentation

The web page snippet focuses on provisioner capabilities and getting started links rather than authentication or configuration details.

input.prompt: Show the configuration file structure for gha-outrunner. Does it require a github section with URL, scope (repo/org), and authentication (token, PAT, GitHub App)? Quote the relevant sections.

content.content:
# gha-outrunner Configuration Structure

The configuration file does **not** require a dedicated GitHub section. Instead, it uses top-level fields for repository/organization access:

## Required Fields

**URL Configuration:**
> "Global repository or organization URL. Applies to all runners that don't set their own `url`."

This can be specified globally or per-runner:
> "Every runner must have a URL reachable via the resolution chain: per-runner `url`, `--url` flag, or this global field."

**Authentication:**
> "Global path to a file containing the GitHub PAT. The file should contain just the token, with optional trailing whitespace/newline."

Token resolution follows a priority chain:
1. Per-runner `token_file`
2. `--token` CLI flag
3. `GITHUB_TOKEN` environment variable
4. systemd-creds path
5. Global `token_file`

## Key Difference from Traditional GitHub Config

The structure doesn't distinguish between repo and org scope—you simply specify the target URL. There's no explicit "GitHub App" authentication option; it relies on Personal Access Tokens (PATs) or environment variables.

input.reasoning:
I attempted to refute the finding and failed on every factual axis; independent evidence actually strengthens it.

Verification against the doc (/tmp/claude-1000/-home-jleechan-projects-worktree-runner/8ca589dd-8422-44b1-a543-52bd7b71f14b/scratchpad/gistfile1.txt):
(1) The words "token", "PAT", "auth", "credential", "registration", and "URL" (in the GitHub-target sense) appear nowhere in the 292-line doc. (2) The generated outrunner.yml in src/config.rs (doc lines 212-251) contains exactly backend/labels/count/resources(/docker limits) — no github section, no url, no token reference; the line citation in the finding is accurate. (3) "Use GitHub Scale Sets + ephemeral runners by default" is indeed Core Principle 3 (doc line 10). (4) The module table (section 4, lines 62-78) has no module owning credentials or registration. (5) `init` (main.rs lines 131-137) asks no repo/org/URL/token question, and runner::start is a placeholder that would invoke `gha-outrunner --config outrunner.yml` with no --url/--token flags.

Strongest refutation candidate — "auth is implicitly delegated to gha-outrunner" — fails on external evidence. gha-outrunner is a real tool (github.com/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner), and its configuration reference (docs/reference/configuration.md) states: "Every runner must have a URL reachable via the resolution chain: per-runner url, --url flag, or this global field," plus a token resolution chain (per-runner token_file → --token → GITHUB_TOKEN env → systemd-creds → global token_file). The wrapper's whole job includes generating outrunner.yml, and the config it generates omits the mandatory url and any token_file, and the design never instructs the user to set GITHUB_TOKEN or pass flags. So the finding's sharpest claim — that `gha-isolated init && gha-isolated start` as designed cannot produce a runner that appears in GitHub — is confirmed by the upstream tool's own docs, not merely plausible. The severity (critical) is proportionate: for a turnkey runner-provisioning CLI whose init/config-generation is a first-class module, omitting the only fields that connect it to GitHub is a core-functionality gap, not a nitpick, and the doc itself claims "This skeleton is runnable today."

One minor caveat on the recommendation (not enough to refute): gha-outrunner currently supports PAT-file/env/token-flag auth only — there is no native GitHub App option per its configuration reference. "GitHub App as primary" is still implementable (the wrapper can mint installation tokens and write them to token_file, refreshing on expiry), but the recommendation should be framed as wrapper-side token minting against gha-outrunner's token_file mechanism rather than a config option that exists upstream. Similarly, since gha-outrunner uses the scaleset API for registration, the "JIT runner config" suggestion is one option among several rather than the obvious fit. The rest of the recommendation (scoping as an init question, token lifecycle, 0600 credential storage outside outrunner.yml/env) aligns directly with gha-outrunner's actual token_file design and is sound.

Sources: [NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner](https://github.com/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner), [gha-outrunner configuration reference](https://github.com/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner/blob/main/docs/reference/configuration.md)
input.corrected_claim: The finding stands as written, with one refinement to the recommendation: gha-outrunner natively supports only PAT-style tokens (token_file / --token / GITHUB_TOKEN / systemd-creds) and a mandatory runner URL — it has no built-in GitHub App auth. The design should therefore specify (a) collecting the target URL and scope at `gha-isolated init`, (b) writing url + token_file into the generated outrunner.yml (or documenting the env/flag path), and (c) if GitHub App auth is desired as primary, wrapper-side installation-token minting/refresh into the token_file path rather than assuming an upstream github: config section.
